abstract: Uncovering the atomic structure of a catalyst is fundamental to identifying the mechanisms driving its performance. Because catalysts rarely maintain their as-synthesized states under operating conditions, structural characterization is best conducted through in situ experimentation. In this study, we employ in situ X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) to investigate Pt3Ni nanoframes, a class of materials recognized for their exceptional electrocatalytic activity toward the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). By probing surface characteristics through electrochemical hydrogen underpotential deposition and carbon monoxide electrooxidation, we observed that surface structural variations directly influence the binding strength of adsorbate molecules. While it is established that the formation of a "Pt-skin" enhances ORR activity by weakening these binding energies, our XAS results provide deeper insight into this transition. We find that nanoframes with stronger adsorbate binding possess rougher surfaces resulting from inadequate Pt segregation and subsequent Ni dissolution. Conversely, nanoframes demonstrating superior ORR activity exhibit pronounced Pt segregation over Ni-rich subsurface layers, facilitating the formation of a high-quality Pt-skin. This work concludes that the remarkable catalytic performance of Pt3Ni hollow nanoframes is primarily dictated by the successful development of this protective and active Pt-skin architecture.
